did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used AI, specifically a two-stage voting ensemble approach combining eight pre-trained deep learning models (including convolutional neural networks and vision transformers) via transfer learning, to classify dermoscopic images for skin cancer diagnosis (melanoma vs. non-melanoma vs. benign).

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and validation of a two-stage voting ensemble AI model using dermoscopic datasets from ISIC and CSMUH Hospital. It includes specific methods (transfer learning, fine-tuning eight pre-trained models), results (reduction in false negatives, specific case counts), and conclusions based on new data analysis, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: AI-Driven Enhancement of Skin Cancer Diagnosis: A Two-Stage Voting Ensemble Approach Using Dermoscopic Data
brief_summary: This study developed an AI diagnostic model using dermoscopic images from the ISIC platform and the CSMUH Hospital dataset to classify skin lesions into melanoma, non-melanoma, and benign categories. The researchers fine-tuned eight pre-trained deep learning models, including convolutional neural networks and vision transformers, and combined the top three performers into a two-stage voting ensemble. The first stage utilized a three-class model for initial classification, while the second stage employed a binary model to re-evaluate benign cases and reduce false negatives. Evaluation on the ISIC dataset showed a reduction in malignant cases misclassified as benign from 124 to 45, while the CSMUH dataset achieved zero false negatives for malignant cases. The results demonstrate that this two-stage classification strategy significantly improves diagnostic accuracy and reduces false negatives, highlighting the potential of AI as a clinical tool for early melanoma detection.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories (melanoma, non-melanoma skin cancer/premalignant, and benign) and binary labels (required treatment vs. no treatment required).
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly describes a 'two-stage classification strategy'. The first stage is a three-class model distinguishing between melanoma (category 2), non-melanoma/premalignant (category 1), and benign (category 0). The second stage is a binary model re-evaluating benign cases to distinguish between 'required treatment' (malignant and premalignant) and 'no treatment required' (benign). The study focuses on reducing false negatives in malignant lesion classification.

datasets_used.name: CSMUH Dataset
datasets_used.description: A private dataset compiled by dermatologists at Chung Shan Medical University Hospital comprising dermoscopic images of skin lesions collected from 2019 to 2023. Photos were obtained from individuals who had skin lesions excised in the dermatology department, with all lesions verified by pathologists. Images were taken using a Canon EOS 500D camera and a HEINE DELTA 20 dermatoscope.
classes_used: Actinic Keratosis (including Bowen’s disease, keratoacanthoma, and squamous cell carcinoma)

use.training: Used for model training and testing. The seven categories were reclassified into three groups (Category 2: Melanoma; Category 1: BCC + AK; Category 0: Others) for the three-class model, and into two groups (Required Treatment vs. No Treatment) for the binary model.
use.testing: Used for testing the two-stage voting ensemble model. The study reported zero false negatives for malignant cases in this dataset.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2017, 2018, 2019, 2020
datasets_used.description: A comprehensive public dataset created by integrating data from the ISIC Archive (ISIC 2018 Task 3, ISIC 2017, ISIC 2018 Task 1, ISIC 2019, and ISIC 2020) to address data imbalance. Duplicate images were removed before combining.

use.training: Used for model training and testing. The seven categories were reclassified into three groups (Category 2: Melanoma; Category 1: BCC + AK; Category 0: Others) for the three-class model, and into two groups (Malignant vs. Benign) for the binary model.
use.testing: Used for testing the two-stage voting ensemble model. The study reported a reduction in malignant cases misclassified as benign from 124 to 45 in the ISIC dataset.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the performance of the three-class and two-stage classification models by displaying true positives, true negatives, false positives, and false negatives for both the ISIC and CSMUH datasets.
To compare the performance of the ensemble model against individual pre-trained models (Swin Transformer, EfficientNet variants) on training and testing sets.
To report the variability of accuracy scores across multiple random experiments for individual models and the ensemble model, ensuring stability.

statistical_methods_used.method: Data Augmentation (random flipping, rotating, translating, scaling)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To increase the amount of training data and bring the training set closer to a balanced distribution of categories, thereby improving model generalization.
To combine predictions from the top three performing models in the first stage and re-evaluate benign cases in the second stage to reduce false negatives.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It reported descriptive statistics such as accuracy, standard deviation, and counts of misclassified cases (e.g., reduction in false negatives from 124 to 45) to demonstrate performance improvements.

three-class_classification.notes: The optimal ensemble model consisted of the Swin Transformer, Vision Transformer, and EfficientNetB5. The study reported that false negatives for malignant cases were completely eliminated (reduced to zero) in the CSMUH dataset using the two-stage classification strategy.

The optimal ensemble model consisted of the Swin Transformer, EfficientNetB5, and EfficientNetV2B2. The study reported that the number of malignant cases misclassified as benign dropped from 124 to 45 in the ISIC dataset using the two-stage classification strategy.

architecture_used.main_model: Two-stage voting ensemble approach combining eight pre-trained deep learning models (Swin Transformer, Vision Transformer (ViT), ResNet50, VGG16, EfficientNetB2, EfficientNetB3, EfficientNetB4, EfficientNetB5, EfficientNetV2B2, EfficientNetV2B3). The optimal ensemble for the three-class model consisted of Swin Transformer, Vision Transformer, and EfficientNetB5 (CSMUH dataset) or Swin Transformer, EfficientNetB5, and EfficientNetV2B2 (ISIC dataset).
architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning using eight pre-trained models (convolutional neural networks and vision transformers).

architecture_details.ensemble_method: Two-stage voting ensemble. Stage 1: Three-class classification (Melanoma, Non-melanoma/premalignant, Benign). Stage 2: Binary classification of benign cases from Stage 1 to reduce false negatives (Required treatment vs. No treatment required).
architecture_details.dropout: Dropout layers were incorporated in each model.

training_details.data_augmentation: Random flipping, rotating, translating, and scaling of dermoscopic images.
training_details.normalization: Images normalized to facilitate deep-learning model computations.
training_details.experiments: Multiple random experiments conducted to ensure stability.

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing all dermoscopic images to 224x224 pixels
Normalization of images to facilitate deep-learning model computations
Data augmentation via random flipping, rotating, translating, and scaling of dermoscopic images
Cropping lesions to the center of the image (CSMUH dataset)

conclusions: The proposed two-stage voting ensemble approach, combining eight pre-trained deep learning models (including convolutional neural networks and vision transformers) via transfer learning, demonstrated clear success in both the ISIC and CSMUH datasets.

A three-class AI model can assist doctors in distinguishing between melanoma patients requiring urgent treatment, non-melanoma skin cancer patients who can be treated later, and benign cases that do not require intervention.

The two-stage classification strategy effectively reduces false negatives in malignant lesions; specifically, it reduced the number of malignant cases misclassified as benign from 124 to 45 in the ISIC dataset and completely eliminated false negatives (reducing misclassified malignant cases to zero) in the CSMUH dataset.

The study highlights the potential of AI technology in skin cancer diagnosis, particularly in resource-limited medical settings, where it could serve as a valuable clinical tool to improve diagnostic accuracy, reduce skin cancer mortality, and decrease healthcare costs.
important_remarks: The CSMUH dataset is relatively small (666 cases), which may limit the model's generalizability across different populations.
The ensemble model has high computational demands, which may limit its application in resource-constrained medical environments.
The study lacks external validation on independent datasets beyond the provided ISIC and CSMUH cohorts.
The CSMUH dataset was collected from a single institution (Chung Shan Medical University Hospital), potentially introducing population or dataset bias.
The paper did not report formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals to support the significance of performance improvements.
The ISIC dataset was constructed by integrating multiple years (2017-2020) and removing duplicates, which may affect the independence of samples compared to standard splits.
The study claims 'zero false negatives' in the CSMUH dataset, but this result is based on a small, single-center validation set, raising concerns about overfitting or selection bias.
